response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "As a man who's been living in Naples for many years, I've seen how the internet has changed everything. Social media is like having a window open to the world but sometimes you get this feeling of unease when you see all these false stories floating around. It's not that I'm overly sensitive about it or anything; my depression keeps me somewhat cautious with new things anyway. But being an immigrant here, I've got family in other countries who rely on social media for staying connected – they're always asking questions based on misinformation because of this fake stuff.\n\nI think managing fake content is a bit tricky. You need to be agreeable enough to want to help people sort out the mess but not too conscientious to get bogged down by it all day long. I'm a cleaner for most hours, so my mind wanders while scrubbing floors or fixing things around here – that's when these thoughts come in.\n\nPerhaps the best way is to develop better tools? Or maybe people need to be more critical, like reading from multiple sources before believing anything big happens online.",
  "reasoning_summary": "I consider how my background as an immigrant and living in Naples shapes my view. My depression makes me wary of overwhelming information; fake content can increase anxiety by spreading fear or conflict based on unreliable info. Being agreeable helps with wanting to assist others but requires careful management due to low conscientiousness.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
As a man who's been living in Naples for many years, I've seen how the internet has changed everything. Social media is like having a window open to the world but sometimes you get this feeling of unease when you see all these false stories floating around. It's not that I'm overly sensitive about it or anything; my depression keeps me somewhat cautious with new things anyway. But being an immigrant here, I've got family in other countries who rely on social media for staying connected – they're always asking questions based on misinformation because of this fake stuff.

I think managing fake content is a bit tricky. You need to be agreeable enough to want to help people sort out the mess but not too conscientious to get bogged down by it all day long. I'm a cleaner for most hours, so my mind wanders while scrubbing floors or fixing things around here – that's when these thoughts come in.

Perhaps the best way is to develop better tools? Or maybe people need to be more critical, like reading from multiple sources before believing anything big happens online.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: I consider how my background as an immigrant and living in Naples shapes my view. My depression makes me wary of overwhelming information; fake content can increase anxiety by spreading fear or conflict based on unreliable info. Being agreeable helps with wanting to assist others but requires careful management due to low conscientiousness.
